text: Posterior retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy, the right surgery for the right patient.
The authors present a case of a sixty-four years old male, with past medical history of Hypertension, medicated with three different antihypertensive drugs.
Rectal resection for rectal cancer, with permanent colostomy, in April two thousand and eighteen.
And bladder cancer, high grade T-one, diagnosed after derp surgery, in June two thousand and nineteen.
During the staging of the bladder cancer, abdominal pelvic CT scan, found a right adrenal gland tumor, with four and a half centimeters.
The patient was totally asymptomatic, and had no findings in physical examination.
The metabolic study, with evaluation of plasmatic and urinary metanephrines, confirmed the presence of a full functioning adrenal gland tumor.
In August, the patient was proposed for a right posterior, retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy.
The patient is positioned in the genopeptoral position with bent hips and knees, preparing for the posterior approach of the adrenalectomy.
The authors chose this method because of the past history of radical abdominal surgery, and the retrocava localization of the tumor.
The surgeon performs a 1.5 cm transverse incision just below the tip of the twelfth rib, preparing the surgical site for the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon reaches the retroperitoneal space by performing blunt and sharp dissection of the abdominal wall, exposing the area for further surgical access.
The surgeon prepares a small cavity using digital manipulation for the insertion of a 5mm trocar, positioned four to five centimeters laterally beneath the eleventh rib, guided by finger palpation.

A ten-millimeter trocar is inserted four to five centimeters medially to the initial incision, positioned below the twelfth rib, using the posterior approach with the patient in the genopectoral position.
A blunt trachea with an inflatable balloon and adjustable sleeve is introduced into the initial incision site and secured in place.
The patient was discharged on the second day after surgery.
No complications were recorded.
One month after surgery, the patient had the first follow-up consultation.
The metabolic study confirmed normalization of both plasmatic and urinary metanephrines.

In conclusion, with this video, the authors demonstrate the feasibility and safety of a successful, posterior retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy, in a patient with past history of radical abdominal surgery.
